name: Sensetable
short_description: A tabletop platform that wirelessly tracks the position and orientation of multiple physical pucks via electromagnetic sensing for tangible user interfaces.
long_description: Sensetable is a tangible user interface platform from the MIT Media Lab's Tangible Media Group that electromagnetically tracks the positions and orientations of multiple wireless objects (pucks) on a flat display surface quickly and without susceptibility to occlusion or lighting changes. Each puck carries digital state that users can modify directly by attaching physical dials and modifiers, and a projector renders graphics around the objects to close the loop between physical manipulation and on-screen simulation. Demonstrated with chemistry and business supply-chain (system dynamics) simulations, it matters to physical AI and interaction design as an influential precedent for tabletop tangible interaction, showing how everyday graspable objects can become the controls of a computational system.
ai_note: Sensetable is an electromagnetic object-tracking and tabletop projection technique with no machine learning; sensing and state-tracking are purely electromagnetic and rule-based.

images.caption: The Sensetable tabletop platform with wireless tracked pucks and projected graphics.
images.alt_text: A flat tabletop surface with several round physical pucks placed on it, surrounded by projected graphical interface elements, used as a tangible interaction platform.

sources.title: Sensetable: A Wireless Object Tracking Platform for Tangible User Interfaces — MIT Media Lab

interactions.title: Drop a puck to grab a piece of data
interactions.description: Place a physical puck anywhere on the table and it binds to a digital object; slide it around and the on-screen data follows it, with projected graphics drawn right beside the puck so the physical token and its digital state stay visually tied together.

interactions.title: Turn a dial on the puck to change a value
interactions.description: Attach a physical dial to a puck and twist it to adjust the bound parameter in real time — the electromagnetic sensing reads the dial's setting so a graspable knob, not a slider on a screen, drives the simulation.

interactions.title: Snap on a token to reassign what a puck does
interactions.description: Press a modifier token onto a puck to change its meaning or the data it represents, then peel it off again — a quick physical way to remap controls mid-task without menus.

interactions.title: Lift a puck to release its data
interactions.description: Pick a puck up off the surface and it lets go of whatever digital object it held, freeing both the token and the state — adding and removing controls is as simple as putting objects down and taking them away.

interactions.title: Build a chemistry simulation by hand
interactions.description: In the demonstrated chemistry application, users arrange pucks standing in for molecules or reaction components on the table and watch projected graphics respond, exploring the simulation by physically rearranging the pieces.

interactions.title: Lay out a supply chain and watch it run
interactions.description: In the system-dynamics business application, several people gather around the table, each placing and turning pucks to set stocks and flow rates of a supply-chain model, and the projected feedback updates as they reshape the system together.

interactions.title: A robust tabletop for messy hands and dim rooms
interactions.description: Because the tracking is electromagnetic rather than camera-based, pucks could be sensed even when hands hover over them or the lighting dips — making it a sturdy surface for a hands-on lab or workshop where people lean in and gesture over the table. Grounded in the paper's stated immunity to occlusion and lighting changes.

interactions.title: Tabletop classroom model that students rearrange
interactions.description: Hand small groups a set of dialed pucks and let them physically build and tune a population, ecosystem, or economics model on the table, learning the dynamics by moving and turning real objects rather than typing parameters — extending the demonstrated multi-user system-dynamics setup to teaching.
